text: The surgeon removes the drain that was passing through the omentum, which obstructed the view of the gallbladder. Due to poor visualization, additional trocars are placed in the epigastrium to elevate the camera for better exposure.

The surgeon has located the gallbladder and is removing omental adhesions while managing obstructive visceral fat using a sponge to improve visibility. The patient is positioned in steep reverse Trendelenburg, with the assistant holding the gallbladder and fat to maintain a clear view. The surgeon begins dissection on the medial aspect of the gallbladder, identifying the artery and aiming to locate the duct.

The surgeon attempts to find a suitable plane behind the gallbladder using a middle-down technique, aiming to identify the duct. Persistent visceral fat obstructs the view, complicating the dissection. The surgeon isolates the artery to improve visualization, seeking to achieve the critical view of safety.

The surgeon thins out the fatty tissue overlying the artery, creating a clear window behind it. The artery is identified and divided, although the critical view is not fully achieved due to the challenging visualization.

The surgeon attempts to dissect behind the gallbladder, facing challenges as adipose tissue obstructs the view. The dissection shifts to a higher position on the gallbladder, adopting a top-down approach due to the relatively uninflamed state of the gallbladder, which facilitates easier manipulation of tissue planes.
The surgeon carefully dissects over the top of the gallbladder, avoiding interference from surrounding fat, and gradually progresses downward to the bottom of the gallbladder.

The surgeon clears fat from the posterior aspect of the duct, revealing the cut edge of the artery. Despite struggling with obstructive fat, the duct is clearly visible, and the surgeon proceeds to divide it, completing the dissection at the bottom of the gallbladder.
